---
title: "Building an R Package with Rust"
subtitle: "The FinancialFunctions Package — 55 Excel-Compatible Financial Functions"
author: "Tor Storli and <br> Claude Sonnet 4.6 (Anthropic's AI assistant)"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "📋 Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
    smooth-scroll: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  eval: false
  echo: true
---

```{=html}
<style>
body { font-family: "Segoe UI", Arial, sans-serif; }

.hero {
  background: linear-gradient(135deg, #1a1a2e 0%, #16213e 50%, #0f3460 100%);
  color: white; padding: 3rem 2rem; border-radius: 12px;
  margin-bottom: 2rem; text-align: center;
}
.hero h1 { font-size: 2.5rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; }
.hero p  { font-size: 1.2rem; opacity: 0.85; }
.badge {
  display: inline-block;
  background: rgba(255,255,255,0.15);
  border: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.3);
  border-radius: 20px; padding: 4px 14px;
  font-size: 0.85rem; margin: 4px;
}
.step-box {
  border-left: 5px solid #0f3460; background: #f8faff;
  padding: 1.2rem 1.5rem; border-radius: 0 10px 10px 0; margin: 1.5rem 0;
}
.step-box h3 { color: #0f3460; margin-top: 0; }
.tip-box {
  background: #e8f5e9; border-left: 5px solid #4caf50;
  padding: 1rem 1.5rem; border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0; margin: 1rem 0;
}
.warn-box {
  background: #fff8e1; border-left: 5px solid #ff9800;
  padding: 1rem 1.5rem; border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0; margin: 1rem 0;
}
.error-box {
  background: #fce4ec; border-left: 5px solid #e91e63;
  padding: 1rem 1.5rem; border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0; margin: 1rem 0;
}
.info-box {
  background: #e3f2fd; border-left: 5px solid #2196f3;
  padding: 1rem 1.5rem; border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0; margin: 1rem 0;
}
.new-box {
  background: #f3e5f5; border-left: 5px solid #9c27b0;
  padding: 1rem 1.5rem; border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0; margin: 1rem 0;
}
.arch {
  display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center;
  flex-wrap: wrap; gap: 0.5rem; padding: 2rem; background: #f8faff;
  border-radius: 12px; margin: 1.5rem 0; font-size: 1.1rem;
}
.arch-box {
  background: #0f3460; color: white; padding: 0.8rem 1.4rem;
  border-radius: 8px; text-align: center; min-width: 140px;
}
.arch-box.rust  { background: #b7410e; }
.arch-box.r     { background: #276DC3; }
.arch-box.excel { background: #217346; }
.arch-arrow { font-size: 1.5rem; color: #0f3460; }
.fn-group {
  background: white; border: 1px solid #e0e0e0;
  border-radius: 8px; padding: 1rem 1.5rem; margin: 0.5rem 0;
}
.fn-group h4 { margin-top: 0; color: #0f3460; }
.fn-pill {
  display: inline-block; background: #e3f2fd; color: #0f3460;
  border-radius: 4px; padding: 2px 8px; font-family: monospace;
  font-size: 0.85rem; margin: 2px;
}
.file-tree {
  background: #1e1e1e; color: #d4d4d4; padding: 1.5rem;
  border-radius: 10px; font-family: "Cascadia Code","Consolas",monospace;
  font-size: 0.9rem; line-height: 1.8;
}
.file-tree .dir  { color: #569cd6; }
.file-tree .new  { color: #4ec9b0; }
.file-tree .copy { color: #dcdcaa; }
.file-tree .gen  { color: #9cdcfe; }
.card-grid {
  display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(200px, 1fr));
  gap: 1rem; margin: 1.5rem 0;
}
.card {
  background: white; border: 1px solid #e0e0e0;
  border-radius: 10px; padding: 1.2rem; text-align: center;
  box-shadow: 0 2px 8px rgba(0,0,0,0.06);
}
.card .icon { font-size: 2rem; margin-bottom: 0.5rem; }
.card .num  { font-size: 2rem; font-weight: 700; color: #0f3460; }
.card .lbl  { font-size: 0.85rem; color: #666; }
</style>
```

::: {.hero}
# 🦀 Building an R Package with Rust

**FinancialFunctions** — From a DuckDB Extension to a Native R Package

::: 
<span class="badge">🦀 Rust</span>
<span class="badge">📦 R Package</span>
<span class="badge">⚡ extendr</span>
<span class="badge">💰 55 Functions</span>
<span class="badge">🪟 Windows 11</span>
:::
:::

---

## 🎯 Overview

This tutorial walks through building **FinancialFunctions** — an R package
that exposes 55 Excel-compatible financial functions implemented in Rust,
achieving native performance while fitting naturally into R workflows.

The key insight: the pure Rust calculation code already existed as a DuckDB
community extension. By swapping the DuckDB binding layer for **extendr**
(the R-Rust bridge), we reuse all 55 financial functions without rewriting
a single line of math.

### Architecture

::: {.arch}
<div class="arch-box excel">📊 Excel<br><small>Reference formulas</small></div>
<div class="arch-arrow">→</div>
<div class="arch-box rust">🦀 Rust calc_*<br><small>Pure math functions</small></div>
<div class="arch-arrow">→</div>
<div class="arch-box" style="background:#6a0dad">⚡ extendr<br><small>#[extendr] macros</small></div>
<div class="arch-arrow">→</div>
<div class="arch-box r">📦 R Package<br><small>library(FinancialFunctions)</small></div>
:::

### What We Build

::: {.card-grid}
<div class="card"><div class="icon">🔢</div><div class="num">55</div><div class="lbl">Financial Functions</div></div>
<div class="card"><div class="icon">📁</div><div class="num">8</div><div class="lbl">Rust Modules</div></div>
<div class="card"><div class="icon">🧪</div><div class="num">59</div><div class="lbl">Unit Tests</div></div>
<div class="card"><div class="icon">⚡</div><div class="num">Rust</div><div class="lbl">Native Speed</div></div>
:::

### The 55 Functions

::: {.fn-group}
#### 💰 Annuity (10)
<span class="fn-pill">fv</span> <span class="fn-pill">pv</span> <span class="fn-pill">pmt</span> <span class="fn-pill">ipmt</span> <span class="fn-pill">ppmt</span> <span class="fn-pill">cumipmt</span> <span class="fn-pill">cumprinc</span> <span class="fn-pill">nper</span> <span class="fn-pill">rate</span> <span class="fn-pill">ispmt</span>
:::
::: {.fn-group}
#### 📈 Cash Flows (5)
<span class="fn-pill">npv</span> <span class="fn-pill">irr</span> <span class="fn-pill">mirr</span> <span class="fn-pill">xnpv</span> <span class="fn-pill">xirr</span>
:::
::: {.fn-group}
#### 🏭 Depreciation (7)
<span class="fn-pill">sln</span> <span class="fn-pill">syd</span> <span class="fn-pill">db</span> <span class="fn-pill">ddb</span> <span class="fn-pill">vdb</span> <span class="fn-pill">amorlinc</span> <span class="fn-pill">amordegrc</span>
:::
::: {.fn-group}
#### 🗓️ Coupon Dates (6)
<span class="fn-pill">coupdaybs</span> <span class="fn-pill">coupdays</span> <span class="fn-pill">coupdaysnc</span> <span class="fn-pill">coupncd</span> <span class="fn-pill">couppcd</span> <span class="fn-pill">coupnum</span>
:::
::: {.fn-group}
#### 🏦 Bonds & Securities (13)
<span class="fn-pill">price</span> <span class="fn-pill">pricedisc</span> <span class="fn-pill">pricemat</span> <span class="fn-pill">yield_</span> <span class="fn-pill">yielddisc</span> <span class="fn-pill">yieldmat</span> <span class="fn-pill">disc</span> <span class="fn-pill">intrate</span> <span class="fn-pill">received</span> <span class="fn-pill">duration</span> <span class="fn-pill">mduration</span> <span class="fn-pill">accrint</span> <span class="fn-pill">accrintm</span>
:::
::: {.fn-group}
#### 🔧 Miscellaneous (14)
<span class="fn-pill">effect</span> <span class="fn-pill">nominal</span> <span class="fn-pill">dollarde</span> <span class="fn-pill">dollarfr</span> <span class="fn-pill">fvschedule</span> <span class="fn-pill">rri</span> <span class="fn-pill">pduration</span> <span class="fn-pill">tbillprice</span> <span class="fn-pill">tbillyield</span> <span class="fn-pill">tbilleq</span> <span class="fn-pill">oddfprice</span> <span class="fn-pill">oddfyield</span> <span class="fn-pill">oddlprice</span> <span class="fn-pill">oddlyield</span>
:::

---

## 🛠️ Prerequisites

### 1️⃣ Install R 4.5+

Download from [CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/).

### 2️⃣ Install Rtools45

Required to compile Rust code on Windows:
[https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/rtools45/rtools.html](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/rtools45/rtools.html)

```r
# Verify Rtools is found
Sys.which("make")
# Should return: C:\rtools45\usr\bin\make.exe
```

### 3️⃣ Install Rust via Rustup

Download from [https://rustup.rs](https://rustup.rs) and run `rustup-init.exe`.

Then add the Windows GNU target — **this is required by R on Windows**:

```bash
rustup target add x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
```

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ If `rustup` is not found in Git Bash, try running it from PowerShell or use the
full path: `~/.cargo/bin/rustup target add x86_64-pc-windows-gnu`
:::

### 4️⃣ Install Required R Packages

```r
install.packages(c("devtools", "usethis", "rextendr", "testthat", "remotes"))
```

::: {.info-box}
ℹ️ On R 4.5 some packages may only be available as source. If you see this
message, install Rtools45 first and retry — it provides the compiler needed
to build them.
:::

### 5️⃣ Configure `.Renviron`

R needs to find the Rust compiler AND the Windows UCRT runtime DLLs.
Open your `.Renviron` file:

```r
usethis::edit_r_environ()
```

Add these lines:

```
PATH="${USERPROFILE}/.cargo/bin;${PATH}"
PATH="C:/rtools45/x86_64-w64-mingw32.static.posix/bin;C:/rtools45/usr/bin;${PATH}"
PATH="C:/Users/YourName/miniconda3/envs/yourenv;${PATH}"
```

::: {.tip-box}
💡 **Why miniconda?** On some Windows systems the UCRT runtime DLLs
(`api-ms-win-crt-*.dll`) needed by Rust live inside a conda environment
rather than `System32`. Find yours with:

```r
shell('where api-ms-win-crt-runtime-l1-1-0.dll 2>&1', intern = TRUE)
```

Add whichever folder it lists to `.Renviron`.
:::

**Restart RStudio** after editing `.Renviron`. Verify Rust is visible:

```r
Sys.which("rustc")
# Should return a path like: C:/Users/YourName/.cargo/bin/rustc.exe
```

---

## 📁 Step 1 — Create the R Package Skeleton

```r
library(usethis)
library(rextendr)

# Create the package folder — opens in a new RStudio project
usethis::create_package("C:/Users/YourName/Desktop/FinancialFunctionsR")
```

### Set Up extendr Scaffolding

Inside the new project:

```r
rextendr::use_extendr()
```

When asked about overwriting files, answer **No** to everything:

| File | Answer |
|------|--------|
| `src/entrypoint.c` | ❌ No |
| `src/Makevars.in` | ❌ No |
| `src/Makevars.win.in` | ❌ No |
| `src/rust/document.rs` | ❌ No |

::: {.info-box}
ℹ️ We say No to all overwrites because we provide our own versions of these
files. `rextendr::use_extendr()` is only needed to update the
`Config/rextendr/version` field in `DESCRIPTION`.
:::

---

## 📂 Step 2 — File Structure

::: {.file-tree}
<pre>
<span class="dir">FinancialFunctionsR/</span>
├── <span class="new">DESCRIPTION</span>            ← Package metadata
├── <span class="new">NAMESPACE</span>              ← R exports (auto-generated by roxygen2)
├── <span class="new">LICENSE</span>
├── <span class="new">README.md</span>
├── <span class="new">_pkgdown.yml</span>           ← pkgdown website config
├── <span class="dir">.github/workflows/</span>
│   ├── <span class="new">pkgdown.yaml</span>       ← Auto-build website on GitHub
│   └── <span class="new">R-CMD-check.yaml</span>   ← Run checks on GitHub
├── <span class="dir">R/</span>
│   ├── <span class="new">extendr-wrappers.R</span> ← .Call() bridges (written manually)
│   └── <span class="new">financial_functions-package.R</span>
├── <span class="dir">vignettes/</span>
│   └── <span class="new">FinancialFunctions.Rmd</span>  ← Getting started guide
├── <span class="dir">src/</span>
│   ├── <span class="new">entrypoint.c</span>
│   ├── <span class="new">Makevars.in</span>
│   ├── <span class="new">Makevars.win.in</span>    ← Template (edit this, not Makevars.win)
│   ├── <span class="gen">Makevars.win</span>       ← Generated at build time — do not edit
│   ├── <span class="new">FinancialFunctions-win.def</span>
│   └── <span class="dir">rust/</span>
│       ├── <span class="new">Cargo.toml</span>
│       └── <span class="dir">src/</span>
│           ├── <span class="new">lib.rs</span>             ← extendr wrappers (NEW)
│           ├── <span class="copy">annuity.rs</span>         ← Copied + DuckDB layer removed
│           ├── <span class="copy">cash_flows.rs</span>      ← Copied + DuckDB layer removed
│           ├── <span class="copy">depreciation.rs</span>    ← Copied + DuckDB layer removed
│           ├── <span class="copy">coupons.rs</span>         ← Copied + DuckDB layer removed
│           ├── <span class="copy">bonds.rs</span>           ← Copied + DuckDB layer removed
│           ├── <span class="copy">misc.rs</span>            ← Copied + DuckDB layer removed
│           ├── <span class="copy">helpers.rs</span>         ← Copied, remove write_f64/read_varchar
│           └── <span class="copy">errors.rs</span>          ← Copied UNCHANGED (pure Rust)
└── <span class="dir">tests/</span>
    ├── testthat.R
    └── <span class="dir">testthat/</span>
        ├── test-annuity.R
        ├── test-cashflows.R
        └── test-other.R
</pre>
:::

---

## ⚙️ Step 3 — Configure Key Files

### `src/rust/Cargo.toml`

```toml
[package]
name = "financial-functions"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["staticlib"]      # staticlib not cdylib — critical on Windows
name = "financial_functions_r"  # linker expects libfinancial_functions_r.a

[dependencies]
extendr-api = "0.7"
financial   = "1.1.5"
chrono      = "0.4"

[profile.release]
lto       = true
strip     = true
opt-level = 3
```

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ **`staticlib` not `cdylib`** is critical. Using `cdylib` produces a
separate `financial_functions_r.dll` that `FinancialFunctions.dll` depends
on at runtime — causing a `LoadLibrary failure` when R tries to load the
package. With `staticlib`, the Rust code is embedded directly into the
package DLL.
:::

### `src/entrypoint.c`

```c
// Forward routine registration from C to Rust.
// Function name must match: R_init_ + PackageName
void R_init_financial_functions_extendr(void *dll);

void R_init_FinancialFunctions(void *dll) {
    R_init_financial_functions_extendr(dll);
}
```

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ Three names must be consistent across three files:

| File | Name |
|------|------|
| `entrypoint.c` | `R_init_FinancialFunctions` |
| `FinancialFunctions-win.def` | `R_init_FinancialFunctions` |
| `extendr_module! { mod ... }` | `financial_functions` (snake_case) |

The C function and DEF file use the R package name. The module uses the
Rust convention (lowercase with underscores).
:::

### `src/FinancialFunctions-win.def`

```
EXPORTS
R_init_FinancialFunctions
```

### `src/Makevars.win.in` — Remove the `document` binary step

Find and delete these lines (edit the `.in` file, not `Makevars.win`):

```makefile
# Generate wrappers           ← delete from here
	export CARGO_HOME=... && \
	export PATH=... && \
	cargo run  --bin document ...   ← to here
```

::: {.tip-box}
💡 `Makevars.win` is regenerated from `Makevars.win.in` on every build.
Editing `Makevars.win` directly is futile — your changes disappear immediately.
Always edit the `.in` template.
:::

### `R/financial_functions-package.R`

```r
#' FinancialFunctions: Excel-Compatible Financial Functions Powered by Rust
#'
#' @docType package
#' @name FinancialFunctions-package
#' @aliases FinancialFunctions
#' @useDynLib FinancialFunctions
"_PACKAGE"
```

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ Do **not** use `@useDynLib FinancialFunctions, .registration = TRUE`.
The `.registration = TRUE` argument causes `getDLLRegisteredRoutines` to fail
because extendr handles registration differently. Use `@useDynLib FinancialFunctions`
with no extra arguments.
:::

---

## 🦀 Step 4 — Copy and Clean the Rust Source Files

Clone your DuckDB extension and copy into `src/rust/src/`:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Tor-Storli/Financial_Functions.git
```

Copy: `annuity.rs`, `cash_flows.rs`, `depreciation.rs`, `coupons.rs`,
`bonds.rs`, `misc.rs`, `helpers.rs`, **`errors.rs`**

### What to Delete from Each File

::: {.error-box}
❌ **DELETE** all DuckDB-specific code. In VS Code, search each file for
`duckdb` — any line containing it must be deleted.

```rust
// DELETE these import lines
use duckdb::core::{DataChunkHandle, LogicalTypeHandle, LogicalTypeId};
use duckdb::vscalar::{ScalarFunctionSignature, VScalar};
use crate::helpers::write_f64;
use crate::helpers::read_varchar;

// DELETE these helper functions
fn dbl() -> LogicalTypeHandle { ... }
fn varchar() -> LogicalTypeHandle { ... }
fn bln() -> LogicalTypeHandle { ... }
fn sig(...) -> ScalarFunctionSignature { ... }
fn fe(e: FinError) -> Box<dyn Error> { ... }

// DELETE all struct and impl blocks
pub struct PmtFunction;
impl VScalar for PmtFunction { ... }

// DELETE all macro invocations
bond_fn!(DurationFunction, ...);
coupon_fn!(CoupdaybsFunction, ...);
scalar_f64!(FvFunction, ...);
```
:::

::: {.tip-box}
✅ **KEEP** all `pub fn calc_*` functions — the pure math stays unchanged.

```rust
pub fn calc_pmt(rate: f64, nper: f64, pv: f64, fv: f64, pmt_at_beg: bool) -> f64 {
    // All the math stays exactly as-is
}
```

The deletion boundary is always the first `macro_rules!`, `pub struct`,
or `impl VScalar` — delete from there to the end of the file.
:::

### Special Cases

**`errors.rs`** — Copy completely **unchanged**. It is 100% pure Rust with
no DuckDB imports. This file defines the `FinError` enum and all validation
helpers.

**`helpers.rs`** — Remove only `write_f64` and `read_varchar`. Keep
`add_months`, `year_frac`, `freq_per_year`, `parse_csv_f64`, `parse_date` etc.

Also remove this unused import if present:
```rust
use std::slice;   // ← delete
```

**`cash_flows.rs`** — The functions called from `lib.rs` must be `pub`.
Also note the actual function names — they differ from the DuckDB version:

```rust
// These five must be pub — add the pub keyword
pub fn npv_calc(rate: f64, values: &[f64]) -> f64 { ... }  // note: npv_calc not calc_npv
pub fn calc_irr(values: &[f64]) -> f64 { ... }
pub fn calc_mirr(values: &[f64], finance_rate: f64, reinvest_rate: f64) -> f64 { ... }
pub fn xnpv_at(values: &[f64], year_fracs: &[f64], rate: f64) -> f64 { ... }  // note: xnpv_at
pub fn calc_xirr(values: &[f64], dates: &[NaiveDate]) -> f64 { ... }

// These stay private — used only internally
fn npv_at_zero(values: &[f64], rate: f64) -> f64 { ... }
fn bisect<F: Fn(f64) -> f64>(...) -> f64 { ... }
```

**`coupons.rs`** — Also remove these DuckDB-specific parsing helpers:

```rust
fn parse_coupon_inputs(...) { ... }         // ← delete
fn parse_coupon_inputs_nobasis(...) { ... } // ← delete
```

**`bonds.rs`** — Remove these helpers:

```rust
fn parse_two_dates(...) { ... }    // ← delete
fn parse_three_dates(...) { ... }  // ← delete
```

**`misc.rs`** — Remove:

```rust
fn parse_two_dates_misc(...) { ... } // ← delete
```

### Update `mod` Declarations in `lib.rs`

```rust
mod helpers;
mod errors;       // ← must be declared — errors.rs is used by other modules
mod annuity;
mod cash_flows;
mod depreciation;
mod coupons;
mod bonds;
mod misc;
```

---

## ⚡ Step 5 — Write `lib.rs` — the extendr Wrappers

This new file replaces all the DuckDB `VScalar` boilerplate with one-line wrappers.

### Top of `lib.rs` — Suppress Warnings

```rust
// Suppress warnings from unused imports left over in copied DuckDB modules
#![allow(unused_imports, dead_code, unused_variables)]

use extendr_api::prelude::*;
use chrono::NaiveDate;

mod helpers;
mod errors;
mod annuity;
mod cash_flows;
mod depreciation;
mod coupons;
mod bonds;
mod misc;

use annuity::*;
use crate::cash_flows::{npv_calc, calc_irr, calc_mirr, xnpv_at, calc_xirr};
use depreciation::*;
use coupons::*;
use bonds::*;
use misc::*;
```

::: {.info-box}
ℹ️ `#![allow(unused_imports, dead_code, unused_variables)]` at the top of
`lib.rs` suppresses the ~49 compiler warnings that come from DuckDB imports
left in the copied files. Without it the package compiles and works correctly
but users see alarming-looking warnings during installation.
:::

### Scalar Functions — 1 Line Each

```rust
#[extendr]
fn pmt(rate: f64, nper: f64, pv: f64, fv: f64, pmt_at_beginning: bool) -> f64 {
    calc_pmt(rate, nper, pv, fv, pmt_at_beginning)
}
```

### Vector Functions — R vectors map to `&[f64]`

```rust
#[extendr]
fn irr(values: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    if values.len() < 2 { return f64::NAN; }
    calc_irr(values)
}
```

### NPV and XNPV — Note the actual function names

```rust
#[extendr]
fn npv(rate: f64, values: &[f64]) -> f64 {
    if values.is_empty() { return f64::NAN; }
    npv_calc(rate, values)   // ← npv_calc, not calc_npv
}

#[extendr]
fn xnpv(rate: f64, values: &[f64], dates: Vec<String>) -> f64 {
    if values.len() != dates.len() || values.is_empty() { return f64::NAN; }
    let parsed: Vec<NaiveDate> = dates.iter()
        .filter_map(|s| NaiveDate::parse_from_str(s.trim(), "%Y-%m-%d").ok())
        .collect();
    if parsed.len() != values.len() { return f64::NAN; }
    let d0 = parsed[0];
    let yf: Vec<f64> = parsed.iter()
        .map(|&d| (d - d0).num_days() as f64 / 365.0)
        .collect();
    xnpv_at(values, &yf, rate)   // ← xnpv_at(values, year_fracs, rate) — note argument order
}
```

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ `xnpv_at` takes arguments in the order `(values, year_fracs, rate)` —
not `(rate, values, year_fracs)`. Getting this wrong causes a Rust compile
error: "arguments to this function are incorrect".
:::

### Date Functions — Accept `Vec<String>`

```rust
#[extendr]
fn xirr(values: &[f64], dates: Vec<String>) -> f64 {
    if values.len() != dates.len() || values.len() < 2 { return f64::NAN; }
    let parsed: Vec<NaiveDate> = dates.iter()
        .filter_map(|s| NaiveDate::parse_from_str(s.trim(), "%Y-%m-%d").ok())
        .collect();
    if parsed.len() != values.len() { return f64::NAN; }
    calc_xirr(values, &parsed)
}
```

### Coupon Functions — Prefix Unused `basis` with `_`

Three coupon functions (`coupncd`, `couppcd`, `coupnum`) accept a `basis`
parameter for API consistency but don't use it. Prefix with `_` to suppress
the warning:

```rust
#[extendr]
fn coupncd(settlement: &str, maturity: &str, frequency: f64, _basis: f64) -> f64 {
    // _basis is intentionally unused — coupncd doesn't depend on day-count convention
}
```

### `extendr_module!` — Register All 55 Functions

```rust
extendr_module! {
    mod financial_functions;
    fn fv; fn pv; fn pmt; fn ipmt; fn ppmt;
    fn cumipmt; fn cumprinc; fn nper; fn rate; fn ispmt;
    fn npv; fn irr; fn mirr; fn xnpv; fn xirr;
    fn sln; fn syd; fn db; fn ddb; fn vdb;
    fn amorlinc; fn amordegrc;
    fn coupdaybs; fn coupdays; fn coupdaysnc;
    fn coupncd; fn couppcd; fn coupnum;
    fn price; fn pricedisc; fn pricemat;
    fn yield_; fn yielddisc; fn yieldmat;
    fn disc; fn intrate; fn received;
    fn duration; fn mduration;
    fn accrint; fn accrintm;
    fn oddfprice; fn oddfyield; fn oddlprice; fn oddlyield;
    fn effect; fn nominal; fn dollarde; fn dollarfr;
    fn fvschedule; fn rri; fn pduration;
    fn tbillprice; fn tbillyield; fn tbilleq;
}
```

---

## 🔧 Step 6 — Write `R/extendr-wrappers.R`

Because we removed the `document` binary step, R wrappers must be written
manually. Each function calls the Rust C symbol by its **quoted name**:

```r
# Generated by extendr: Do not edit by hand
# nolint start
#' @usage NULL
#' @useDynLib FinancialFunctions
NULL

#' Periodic Payment
#' @export
pmt <- function(rate, nper, pv, fv, pmt_at_beginning) {
    .Call("wrap__pmt", rate, nper, pv, fv, pmt_at_beginning)
}

#' Internal Rate of Return
#' @export
irr <- function(values) .Call("wrap__irr", values)

#' IRR with Irregular Dates
#' @export
xirr <- function(values, dates) .Call("wrap__xirr", values, dates)

# ... all 55 functions
# nolint end
```

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ **Quotes are mandatory.** `.Call("wrap__pmt", ...)` works.
`.Call(wrap__pmt, ...)` without quotes fails with
`Error: object 'wrap__pmt' not found` because R looks for an R object
named `wrap__pmt` — which doesn't exist.
:::

### Verify Symbol Names Before Writing

After a successful build, confirm the exact exported symbol names:

```r
shell('objdump -t "src/FinancialFunctions.dll" 2>&1', intern = TRUE) |>
    grep("wrap__", x = _, value = TRUE)
```

All 55 `wrap__*` symbols should appear. If any are missing, check the
`extendr_module!` block in `lib.rs`.

### Add Titles So pkgdown Can Index Functions

Every function needs at least a title line for pkgdown to recognise it:

```r
#' Future Value
#' @export
fv <- function(...) .Call("wrap__fv", ...)

#' Present Value
#' @export
pv <- function(...) .Call("wrap__pv", ...)
```

---

## 🏗️ Step 7 — Build the Package

### First Build

```r
devtools::document()   # Compiles Rust + generates R documentation
devtools::load_all()   # Loads into current R session
```

The first compile downloads all Cargo dependencies and takes 2–5 minutes.
Subsequent incremental builds complete in under 30 seconds.

### Verify It Works

```r
pmt(0.0325/12, 180, 350000, 0, FALSE)      # -2459.341
irr(c(-70000, 12000, 15000, 18000, 21000, 26000))   # 0.0866
price("2008-02-15", "2017-11-15", 0.0575, 0.065, 100, 2, 0)  # 94.63
xirr(c(-10000, 2750, 4250, 3250, 2750),
     c("2008-01-01","2008-03-01","2008-10-30","2009-02-15","2009-04-01"))  # 0.3734
```

---

## 🧪 Step 8 — Run the Test Suite

```r
devtools::test()
```

Expected output:

```
✓ | OK F W S | Context
✓ | 22       | annuity functions [1.2s]
✓ | 12       | cash flow functions [0.8s]
✓ | 25       | other functions [0.5s]

[ FAIL 0 | WARN 0 | SKIP 0 | PASS 59 ]
```

---

## 📖 Step 9 — Add a Vignette

A vignette gives users a long-form guide with examples of every function group.

```r
# Create the vignette
usethis::use_vignette("FinancialFunctions",
                       title = "Getting Started with FinancialFunctions")

# Add required packages to DESCRIPTION
usethis::use_package("knitr",     type = "Suggests")
usethis::use_package("rmarkdown", type = "Suggests")
```

Replace the generated `vignettes/FinancialFunctions.Rmd` with your
comprehensive guide covering all 55 functions.

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ Functions like `ipmt`, `ppmt`, `syd`, `ddb` accept scalar `per` arguments —
they do not accept integer vectors. When building tables inside a vignette,
use `sapply()`:

```r
# WRONG — passes an integer vector to a scalar argument
ipmt(0.04/12, 1:12, 360, 300000, 0, FALSE)

# CORRECT — iterates with sapply
sapply(1:12, function(p) ipmt(0.04/12, p, 360, 300000, 0, FALSE))
```
:::

---

## 🌐 Step 10 — Set Up pkgdown Website

pkgdown builds a full documentation website from your R docs and vignette.

```r
install.packages("pkgdown")
usethis::use_pkgdown()   # creates _pkgdown.yml
```

Configure `_pkgdown.yml` to group functions by category with icons in the
function reference:

```yaml
url: https://tor-storli.github.io/FinancialFunctionsR/

template:
  bootstrap: 5
  bootswatch: cosmo

reference:
  - title: "💰 Annuity Functions"
    contents: [fv, pv, pmt, ipmt, ppmt, cumipmt, cumprinc, nper, rate, ispmt]

  - title: "📈 Cash Flow Functions"
    contents: [npv, irr, mirr, xnpv, xirr]

  - title: "🏭 Depreciation Functions"
    contents: [sln, syd, db, ddb, vdb, amorlinc, amordegrc]

  - title: "🗓️ Coupon Date Functions"
    contents: [coupdaybs, coupdays, coupdaysnc, coupncd, couppcd, coupnum]

  - title: "🏦 Bond & Security Functions"
    contents: [price, pricedisc, pricemat, yield_, yielddisc, yieldmat,
               disc, intrate, received, duration, mduration,
               accrint, accrintm, oddfprice, oddfyield, oddlprice, oddlyield]

  - title: "🔧 Miscellaneous Functions"
    contents: [effect, nominal, dollarde, dollarfr, fvschedule, rri,
               pduration, tbillprice, tbillyield, tbilleq]
```

### Build the Site Locally

```r
options(pkgdown.internet = FALSE)  # avoids network errors behind proxies
pkgdown::build_site()
```

---

## 🚀 Step 11 — Publish to GitHub

### Create the Repository First

1. Go to **https://github.com/Tor-Storli**
2. Click **New** → name it `FinancialFunctionsR`
3. ⚠️ Leave **all checkboxes unchecked** — the repo must be completely empty

### Configure `.gitignore`

Your repo should already have a `.gitignore`. Make sure it includes:

```
src/rust/target/    # Rust build artifacts — can be 1-2 GB
src/Makevars.win    # Auto-generated — never commit
.Rproj.user
src/rust/vendor
```

Add `src/rust/target/` if missing:

```bash
echo "src/rust/target/" >> .gitignore
```

### Push to GitHub (RStudio Terminal)

```bash
# 1. Initialise git
git init

# 2. Set identity (first time only)
git config --global user.name "Tor Storli"
git config --global user.email "your@email.com"

# 3. Stage all files
git add .

# 4. First commit
git commit -m "Initial release — 55 Excel financial functions in Rust"

# 5. Rename branch to main
git branch -M main

# 6. Connect to GitHub
git remote add origin https://github.com/Tor-Storli/FinancialFunctionsR.git

# 7. Push
git push -u origin main
```

::: {.tip-box}
💡 When Git asks for credentials use your GitHub username and a
**Personal Access Token** (not your password).
Generate one at: **GitHub → Settings → Developer Settings → Personal Access Tokens → Classic**
with the `repo` scope checked.
:::

::: {.warn-box}
⚠️ **SSL certificate error on Windows corporate networks:**

```bash
# Fix by switching to the Windows certificate store
git config --global http.sslBackend schannel
git push
```
:::

### Fix `configure` File Permissions for GitHub Actions

::: {.new-box}
🆕 **Windows-specific issue:** Git on Windows doesn't track Unix executable
permissions. The `configure` file loses its executable bit when pushed, causing
GitHub Actions to fail with:

```
ERROR: 'configure' exists but is not executable
```

Fix with (runs from Windows — no Unix needed):

```bash
git update-index --chmod=+x configure
git update-index --chmod=+x configure.win
git add configure configure.win
git commit -m "Fix configure file permissions for GitHub Actions"
git push
```
:::

---

## ⚙️ Step 12 — GitHub Actions (CI/CD)

### R-CMD-check Badge

```r
usethis::use_github_action("check-standard")
```

This creates `.github/workflows/R-CMD-check.yaml` and enables the green
✅ badge on your README.

### pkgdown Auto-Deploy

Create `.github/workflows/pkgdown.yaml`:

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  workflow_dispatch:

name: pkgdown
permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  pkgdown:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      GITHUB_PAT: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-pandoc@v2
      - uses: r-lib/actions/setup-r@v2
        with:
          use-public-rspm: true
      - name: Install Rust
        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
      - name: Install system dependencies
        run: sudo apt-get install -y libssl-dev libcurl4-openssl-dev libxml2-dev
      - name: Install R dependencies
        run: install.packages(c("pkgdown","remotes","rmarkdown","knitr","devtools"))
        shell: Rscript {0}
      - name: Build package
        run: R CMD build --no-manual .
      - name: Install package
        run: |
          pkg <- list.files(".", pattern="\\.tar\\.gz$", full.names=TRUE)[1]
          install.packages(pkg, repos=NULL, type="source")
        shell: Rscript {0}
      - name: Build pkgdown site
        run: |
          options(pkgdown.internet = FALSE)
          pkgdown::build_site_github_pages(new_process=FALSE, install=FALSE)
        shell: Rscript {0}
      - name: Deploy to GitHub Pages
        if: github.event_name != 'pull_request'
        uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@v4.5.0
        with:
          clean: false
          branch: gh-pages
          folder: docs
```

::: {.info-box}
ℹ️ **Why not use `extra-packages: local::.`?** The standard `r-lib/actions/setup-r-dependencies`
action with `local::.` fails for Rust packages because it tries to install your
package before Rust is configured. The manual approach above — build, then
install the `.tar.gz`, then run pkgdown — works reliably.
:::

Enable GitHub Pages: **Repo → Settings → Pages → Source → GitHub Actions**

Your site will be live at:
```
https://tor-storli.github.io/FinancialFunctionsR/
```

---

## 🐛 Troubleshooting Guide

### 🔴 `rustc not found` in RStudio

```r
usethis::edit_r_environ()
# Add: PATH="${USERPROFILE}/.cargo/bin;${PATH}"
# Restart RStudio
```

### 🔴 `rustup: command not found` in Git Bash

```powershell
# Run from PowerShell instead
rustup target add x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
```

### 🔴 `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu target not installed`

```bash
rustup target add x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
```

### 🔴 `cannot find -lfinancial_functions_r`

In `Cargo.toml`:
```toml
[lib]
crate-type = ["staticlib"]
name = "financial_functions_r"
```

### 🔴 `LoadLibrary failure: The specified module could not be found`

The UCRT DLLs aren't on R's PATH. Locate them:

```r
shell('where api-ms-win-crt-runtime-l1-1-0.dll 2>&1', intern = TRUE)
```

Add the folder to `.Renviron` and restart RStudio.

### 🔴 `financial_functions_r.dll` dependency in built DLL

You have an old `cdylib` artifact cached. Delete and rebuild:

```r
unlink("src/rust/target", recursive = TRUE)
file.remove("src/FinancialFunctions.dll")
devtools::document()
```

### 🔴 `no bin target named 'document'`

Edit `src/Makevars.win.in` (not `Makevars.win`) and remove the lines
containing `cargo run --bin document`.

### 🔴 `object 'wrap__pmt' not found`

Add quotes: `.Call("wrap__pmt", ...)` not `.Call(wrap__pmt, ...)`.

### 🔴 `must specify DLL via a "DLLInfo" object`

Check `NAMESPACE` has exactly one `useDynLib` line without `.registration = TRUE`:

```r
useDynLib(FinancialFunctions)
exportPattern("^[^\\.]")
```

Also check `R/extendr-wrappers.R` — the `@useDynLib` tag must not have
`.registration = TRUE`.

### 🔴 `unresolved import crate::errors`

Add `mod errors;` to `lib.rs` module declarations.

### 🔴 `unresolved imports crate::cash_flows::calc_npv, calc_xnpv`

The actual function names differ from what you might expect. Use:

```rust
use crate::cash_flows::{npv_calc, calc_irr, calc_mirr, xnpv_at, calc_xirr};
//                       ^^^^^^^^                       ^^^^^^^
//                       not calc_npv                   not calc_xnpv
```

### 🔴 `arguments to this function are incorrect` for `xnpv_at`

The argument order is `xnpv_at(values, year_fracs, rate)` — not
`xnpv_at(rate, values, year_fracs)`.

### 🔴 Compiler warnings about unused imports

Add to the top of `lib.rs`:

```rust
#![allow(unused_imports, dead_code, unused_variables)]
```

### 🔴 `ERROR: 'configure' exists but is not executable` (GitHub Actions)

```bash
git update-index --chmod=+x configure
git update-index --chmod=+x configure.win
git add configure configure.win
git commit -m "Fix configure permissions"
git push
```

### 🔴 `setup-r-dependencies` fails in pkgdown GitHub Action

Don't use `extra-packages: local::.` for Rust packages. Build and install
manually — see the pkgdown workflow in Step 12.

### 🔴 `SSL certificate problem` on `git push`

```bash
git config --global http.sslBackend schannel
git push
```

### 🔴 `rextendr::document()` deprecated warning

Use `devtools::document()` instead. The `rextendr::document()` function was
deprecated in rextendr 0.4.0.

---

## 📊 Key Concepts Summary

### DuckDB vs R — What Changes

| Aspect | DuckDB Extension | R Package |
|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Binding crate** | `duckdb` | `extendr-api` |
| **Function wrapper** | `impl VScalar` (~30 lines) | `#[extendr]` (1 line) |
| **Calc functions** | ✅ Unchanged | ✅ Unchanged |
| **CSV string inputs** | `irr('-70000,12000')` | `irr(c(-70000, 12000))` |
| **Date inputs** | CSV string | `Vec<String>` parsed in wrapper |
| **Bad input** | Returns `NULL` | Returns `NA` (`f64::NAN`) |
| **Library type** | `cdylib` | `staticlib` |
| **`errors.rs`** | Actively used | Copied unchanged — still needed |
| **`write_f64`** | Required | Delete — not needed |
| **`read_varchar`** | Required | Delete — not needed |

### The extendr Macro Magic

```rust
#[extendr]                                  // ← This single attribute...
fn pmt(rate: f64, nper: f64, ...) -> f64 { // ← ...generates all C glue
    calc_pmt(rate, nper, ...)               // ← Pure math, unchanged
}
```

The `#[extendr]` macro automatically:

- Generates `wrap__pmt` C entry point
- Handles R ↔ Rust type conversion
- Converts `f64::NAN` → R `NA`
- Registers the function via `extendr_module!`

---

## 🎓 Further Reading

- **extendr:** [https://extendr.github.io](https://extendr.github.io)
- **rextendr:** [https://extendr.github.io/rextendr](https://extendr.github.io/rextendr)
- **DuckDB extension:** [https://github.com/Tor-Storli/Financial_Functions](https://github.com/Tor-Storli/Financial_Functions)
- **R package:** [https://github.com/Tor-Storli/FinancialFunctionsR](https://github.com/Tor-Storli/FinancialFunctionsR)
- **Package website:** [https://tor-storli.github.io/FinancialFunctionsR](https://tor-storli.github.io/FinancialFunctionsR)
- **Rustup:** [https://rustup.rs](https://rustup.rs)

---

::: {.hero}
### 🎉 You Did It!

You built a production-grade R package with 55 Excel-compatible financial
functions running at native Rust speed — reusing every line of calculation
code from your DuckDB extension, publishing it to GitHub, and deploying
a full documentation website.

**The key takeaway:** extendr makes the Rust → R boundary almost invisible.
The hard work is the math. Write it once in Rust, deploy it everywhere.

<span class="badge">🦀 Rust powers the math</span>
<span class="badge">⚡ extendr bridges to R</span>
<span class="badge">📦 R packages it up</span>
<span class="badge">🌐 pkgdown publishes it</span>
<span class="badge">🤖 GitHub Actions maintains it</span>
:::
